EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A merger of two thunderstorms produced a brief funnel cloud visible from Benton City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public posted a picture of a funnel cloud to social media. Weak rotation verified by RADAR.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mt. Baker observation showed 19 inches in 24 hours, 24 inches total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a strong low pressure system off the coast of Victoria Island brought strong, gusty winds to the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Dissappointment recorded sustained winds up to 53 mph with wind gusts up to 68 mph. Another weather station on Megler Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 48 mph with gusts up to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with abundant moisture welled up from south to north over eastern Washington bringing snow and freezing rain beginning during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8th. Heavy snow was noted in the East Slopes of the Cascades with moderate to locally heavy accumulations over the mountains north of the Columbia Basin. In the basin and the valleys of the northeast mountains initial light snow turned to freezing rain from the Moses Lake area to the Deer Park area during the afternoon and evening hours with generally around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulation and locally higher amounts, creating slick driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grant County Airport ASOS sensor recorded 0.05 inches of freezing rain accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Moses lake reported 0.08 inches of ice accumulation from freezing rain and his driveway a sheet of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the afternoon of January 22nd and continuing through much of the night a cold low pressure system tracked through the region bringing widespread heavy snow to the mountains and valleys of eastern Washington as well as the northern rim of the Columbia Basin. Moderate to locally heavy snow accumulations were noted in the lower elevations of the northern and eastern basin and Okanogan Valley as this storm system passed through the region. The lowest elevations and farthest south reaches of the Columbia Basin at Wenatchee and the Lewiston/Clarkston area received mainly rain from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the Public 6 miles east of Orient reported 7.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 90 was closed in both directions for much of the day January 4th between North Bend and Ellensburg as a result of several multi-vehicle crashes that occurred prior to sunrise  including 1 fatality (indirect). A mix of rain and snow that fell earlier in the night left a wet surface as temperatures fell to just below freezing near daybreak creating treacherous driving conditions.
A warm front brought heavy snow to the Blue Mountains during the evening of January 22nd with 12 hour accumulations between 8 and 10 inches.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air trapped in the upper reaches of the Yakima and Kittitas Valleys with warm air overspreading aloft brought pockets of freezing rain to the area. In Naches, an empty school bus slid off the road. At the accident scene first responders had difficulty standing up on the ice covered road surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain in Naches. A school bus slid off the highway. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the evening of January 6th through the early morning of January 7th an area of persistent snow showers developed over a localized area of northeastern Washington in an unstable air mass behind a cold front. Scattered reports of heavy snow accumulations were received from locations between Springdale and Newport underneath these showers.|Outside of this narrow corridor generally a trace to 1 inch of snow accumulation was noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Newport recorded 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft were transported to the surface via mountain wave action resulting in isolated very windy conditions near the Cascade crest and along the more exposed ridge tops of the Simcoe Highlands. The Highbridge RAWS along Highway 97 measured a gust of 62 knots (71 mph) at an elevation of 2106 feet while a sensor above White Pass measured a gust of 98 knots (113 mph) at 5970 feet. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the afternoon of January 22nd and continuing through much of the night a cold low pressure system tracked through the region bringing widespread heavy snow to the mountains and valleys of eastern Washington as well as the northern rim of the Columbia Basin. Moderate to locally heavy snow accumulations were noted in the lower elevations of the northern and eastern basin and Okanogan Valley as this storm system passed through the region. The lowest elevations and farthest south reaches of the Columbia Basin at Wenatchee and the Lewiston/Clarkston area received mainly rain from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Boundary Dam recorded 5.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with abundant moisture welled up from south to north over eastern Washington bringing snow and freezing rain beginning during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8th. Heavy snow was noted in the East Slopes of the Cascades with moderate to locally heavy accumulations over the mountains north of the Columbia Basin. In the basin and the valleys of the northeast mountains initial light snow turned to freezing rain from the Moses Lake area to the Deer Park area during the afternoon and evening hours with generally around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulation and locally higher amounts, creating slick driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 5 miles northwest of Ardenvoir recorded 7.6 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the northernwestern Blue Mountain and Blue Mountain foothills. Accumulations in the mountains ranged between 10 and 14 inches with 3 to 6 inches in the Blue Mountain foothills with the highest accumulations near Dayton.

A pair of storm systems brought significant snow to all elevations on the 3rd and 4th of February. Wraparound moisture from the first system brought 8 to 12 inches of snow to the Blue Mountains. Initial precipitation with the second system combined with lingering wraparound moisture brought between 3 and 13 inches to all elevations on the 4th of February. Over 200 accidents were reported due to slippery conditions. Interstate 82 between Yakima and Ellensburg was closed for an hour to clear multiple accidents.

A storm brought a mix of wintry precipitation to the region through the day on the 14th of February. Warm air aloft was primarily confined to Benton, Walla Walla and Franklin counties where a light coating of freezing rain fell followed by light snow. Accumulations in these ranges ranged from trace ice to around a tenth of an inch and up to 2 inches of snow. Further west, Klickitat, Yakima and Kittitas County saw mostly snow with total accumulations between 3 and 8 inches.

A upper-level trough generated a few stronger showers up across southwest Washington.  One of these showers produced a downburst of winds, enough to bring down a private plane causing two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private aircraft accident was reported to the Portland TRACON around 4:48 PM on April 29th.  A nearby spotter reported gusty microburst type winds that broke 1-1.5 inch branches off trees east of I-5.  A strong downburst from a nearby shower possibly caused the airplane accident.  Two fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to much of central and eastern Washington beginning on the evening of the 8th and peaking on the 9th of February. Along and in the lee of more exposed ridges in the Yakima and Kittitas Valleys and along the Horse Heaven hills blizzard conditions were observed with sustained winds between 35 and 40 mph (30 to 35 knots) and observed visibilities near zero.   Snow drifts in the Richland area as high as 5 feet were reported with some secondary roads remaining impassable for days. I-90 from Ellensburg to Vantage, I-82 from Yakima to Ellensburg and I-82 from south of the Tri-Cities to the Oregon border were all closed for significant portions of the day due to massive snow drifts and near zero visibility.  Across the region over 500 additional motor vehicle accidents were reported by the Washington State Patrol. In the Yakima Valley impassable roads and harsh conditions resulted in the loss of over 1700 head of cattle at an estimated value of 2.2 million dollars. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches with 5 to 6 inches in the Tri-Cities, 5 to 7 inches in Yakima, 6 to 12 inches in Ellensburg and 5 to 10 inches in the Simcoe Highlands. Accurate snowfall measurements were very difficult due to blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm lasting from the 10th to the 12th of February brought extreme snowfall to the east slopes of the Washington Cascades and Columbia River Gorge with heavy snowfall for the Columbia Basin, Simcoe Highlands and Blue Mountains. Snowfall totals at Snoqualmie Pass neared four and a half feet over 48 hours resulting in the pass being closed for over 72 hours for snow removal and avalanche mitigation. Just after the initial closure a fatal motor vehicle accident (indirect) occurred near Hyak when a tractor trailer crashed into a concrete barrier. Over 300 vehicles became stranded on the pass when the interstate was shut down. They were eventually led to safety on the 12th of February in a Washington State DOT and State Police convoy. Additional storm total snowfall accumulations of 38 inches at Trout Lake, 15 to 22 inches in the Columbia River Gorge, 8 to 12 inches in the Simcoe Highlands, 10 to 12 inches in the Northwest Blue Mountains and 4 to 6 inches in the Blue Mountain foothills were recorded.

A winter storm lasting from the 10th to the 12th of February brought extreme snowfall to the east slopes of the Washington Cascades and Columbia River Gorge with heavy snowfall for the Columbia Basin, Simcoe Highlands and Blue Mountains. Snowfall totals at Snoqualmie Pass neared four and a half feet over 48 hours resulting in the pass being closed for over 72 hours for snow removal and avalanche mitigation. Just after the initial closure a fatal motor vehicle accident (indirect) occurred near Hyak when a tractor trailer crashed into a concrete barrier. Over 300 vehicles became stranded on the pass when the interstate was shut down. They were eventually led to safety on the 12th of February in a Washington State DOT and State Police convoy. Additional storm total snowfall accumulations of 38 inches at Trout Lake, 15 to 22 inches in the Columbia River Gorge, 8 to 12 inches in the Simcoe Highlands, 10 to 12 inches in the Northwest Blue Mountains and 4 to 6 inches in the Blue Mountain foothills were recorded.

A pair of storm systems brought significant snow to all elevations on the 3rd and 4th of February. Wraparound moisture from the first system brought 8 to 12 inches of snow to the Blue Mountains. Initial precipitation with the second system combined with lingering wraparound moisture brought between 3 and 13 inches to all elevations on the 4th of February. Over 200 accidents were reported due to slippery conditions. Interstate 82 between Yakima and Ellensburg was closed for an hour to clear multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed (indirect) and another injured (indirect) in a six vehicle crash on Interstate 82 three miles north of Selah. The cars were traveling east along the interstate during a snow storm and ran into each other as the drivers attempted to slow for an accident ahead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington State Troopers reported a single vehicle rollover on Highway 395 north of Pasco due to speed and slippery road conditions. A 7 year old passenger was killed (indirect) in the crash. They were not wearing a seat belt.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Cascades on the evening on March 11 and continued through the day on March 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Cascades at Paradise at Mt Rainier. Snowfall of 20 inches fell over a period of 24 hours from March 11 thru March 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Basin of eastern Washington is a broad expanse of farm and range land with few trees to interrupt winds. A series of winter storm systems bringing light fluffy snow to the region during the last week of February set up conditions where gusty northeast winds on the exposed terrain of the Columbia Basin lead to persistently drifting snow closing several roads in the area to traffic with road crews unable to keep the roads open. These conditions with periodic road closures continued through the first week of March.  ||Snow amounts and wind speeds did not meet Blizzard criteria during this period but travel on secondary roads in the eastern Columbia Basin was repeatedly disrupted with drifting snow often cutting roads soon after highway crews cleared lanes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of State Route 27 between Garfield and Fairfield were closed due to multiple drifts across the roadway as a result of fresh dry snow and northeast winds gusting 30 to 40 mph. Some of these drifts were 12 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of March 11th and lasting through the afternoon of March 12th a moist slow moving cold front passed through eastern Washington. The northeast mountains and the northern Columbia Basin received heavy snow accumulations from this storm system while locations farther south in the Columbia Basin and west in the Okanogan area generally received 2 to 4 inches with locally higher amounts. The eastern slopes of the Cascades and the far western Columbia Basin benefited from downslope drying as the front passed through with only minor accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Boundary Dam recorded 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of RD 19-NW near Soap Lake was temporarily closed due to snow melt flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool late season system brought one last gasp of wintry weather to the Northwestern Blue Mountains from 10 to 11 April. Storm total snowfall estimated by Snow Telemetry sites was around 10 inches.

Beginning on April 6th and continuing through April 10th a series of Pacific atmospheric rivers were directed into Oregon, southeast Washington and central Idaho saturating the ground with heavy rainfall. During this 4 day period the COOP station at Kamiah, ID reported 1.86 inches of rain, Elk River, ID reported 2.94 inches of rain, Moscow, ID 3.44 inches, Potlatch 2.46 inches, Dworshack Fish Hatchery 3.08 inches , with the ASOS at Pullman, WA recording 2.88 inches of rain. By the 9th of April a rain storm during the afternoon and evening hours overwhelmed the saturated soil and resulted in widespread areal flooding issues from swollen streams and rivers.||During the evening of April 9th the city of Pullman experienced Flash Flooding as heavy rain to the north of the city drained down the Missouri Flat Creek drainage. The business district along North Grand Avenue was inundated with multiple feet of water which damaged numerous businesses and floated cars form parking lots along the road. ||The Missouri Flat Creek flood then drained into the South Fork of the Palouse River in downtown Pullman which was already rising and would peak just at Flood Stage later in the evening with minimal further damage along the river bottom lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Flat Creek experienced a Flash Flood which resulted in the North Grand Avenue business district being inundated with up to 4 feet of water in places. Numerous businesses were damaged and parked cars were floated and carried away. Pullman police and fire crews made numerous water rescues of stranded customers and employees of at least 4 businesses including the rescue of 16 people from one business.  There were no known injuries and by the next morning the creek had receded back into it's banks.

The business district along North Grand Avenue was inundated with multiple feet of water which damaged numerous businesses and floated cars form parking lots along the road. ||The Missouri Flat Creek flood then drained into the South Fork of the Palouse River in downtown Pullman which was already rising and would peak just at Flood Stage later in the evening with minimal further damage along the river bottom lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the South Fork of the Palouse River at Pullman recorded a rise to the the Flood Stage of 7.0 feet at 10:30 PM PDT on April 9th. The river level stabilized and fluctuated just at or slightly below Flood Stage until 4:00 AM April 10th and then receded.||This was a minor and short lived flood event which mainly impacted bottom lands and pedestrian walkways through park land along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and windy cold front swept across eastern Washington during the afternoon of April 27th. Across the Columbia Basin sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph with recorded |gusts as high as 46 mph promoted areas of blowing dust in the western basin. In |the Spokane area two trees were toppled onto homes. These were isolated events probably caused by local terrain and random mixing of stronger winds aloft down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media photographed and broadcast on social media a large tree pushed over onto a house in central Spokane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall total for April produced river flooding along the Walla Walla River and tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on Titus Creek, stemming from log jam on Mill Creek that pushed more water into Titus Creek. As a result of high water on Titus Creek, a tractor was flooded. The Touchet River, 1.5 to 3 miles east of Waitsburg, changed course and began channeling water against a resident's property. The resulting erosion caused two outbuilding to topple into the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A marginally severe thunderstorm produced hail ranging from half inch to one inch near Benton City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public posted a video showing hail with a maximum size of 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning the afternoon of February 14th and continuing through the night into the morning of the 15th a moist warm front moved south to north through eastern Washington. Initial light snow changed over to rain with the warm front passage in the Columbia Basin and the Cascades East Slopes during the evening hours with generally 1 to locally 4 inch snow accumulations before this change-over. The warm air tongue did not penetrate the northeastern mountains during the night with heavy snow accumulations noted in Stevens and Pend Oreille Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Clayton recorded 4.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of February 19th through the morning of February 20th portions of southeast Washington came under a band of wrap-around light snow from a low pressure system passing through Oregon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Clarkston reported 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front developed over central and southeastern Washington during the day on February 10th with light snow through much of the day bringing generally moderate accumulations to the Columbia Basin and Cascades east slopes. A trailing cold front   created a period of more intense snow as it passed through eastern Washington from west to east during the night of February 10th.  These two fronts left a swath of heavy snow accumulation across the northern Columbia Basin and the rising terrain of the eastern Columbia Basin from Spokane southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Spokane International Airport recorded 7.5 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent troughing off the coast of the Pacific Northwest focused a stream of mid-level moisture over the Inland Northwest resulting in a long duration snow event as the plume drifted north and south several times between the 22nd and 25th of February. Breezy northeastern winds in the lower Columbia Basin and Yakima Valley, especially on ridge tops resulting in drifts nearing 5 feet in height making many roads over the ridge tops impassable for several days. Storm total snow accumulations were measured at 25.2 inches in Snowden, 16.5 inches in White Salmon, 10 inches in Ellensburg, 10 inches in Trout Lake, 8 inches in Richland, 9 inches in Walla Walla, 8 inches in Kennewick and 6 inches in Yakima.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to much of central and eastern Washington beginning on the evening of the 8th and peaking on the 9th of February. Along and in the lee of more exposed ridges in the Yakima and Kittitas Valleys and along the Horse Heaven hills blizzard conditions were observed with sustained winds between 35 and 40 mph (30 to 35 knots) and observed visibilities near zero.   Snow drifts in the Richland area as high as 5 feet were reported with some secondary roads remaining impassable for days. I-90 from Ellensburg to Vantage, I-82 from Yakima to Ellensburg and I-82 from south of the Tri-Cities to the Oregon border were all closed for significant portions of the day due to massive snow drifts and near zero visibility.  Across the region over 500 additional motor vehicle accidents were reported by the Washington State Patrol. In the Yakima Valley impassable roads and harsh conditions resulted in the loss of over 1700 head of cattle at an estimated value of 2.2 million dollars. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches with 5 to 6 inches in the Tri-Cities, 5 to 7 inches in Yakima, 6 to 12 inches in Ellensburg and 5 to 10 inches in the Simcoe Highlands. Accurate snowfall measurements were very difficult due to blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility and sustained winds of 35 mph created treacherous conditions across the Yakima Valley. 1800 head of cattle valued at an estimated 2.5 million dollars perished in the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of February 3rd southeast Washington and Central Idaho came under the precipitation shield from a low pressure system migrating through Oregon. This resulted in heavy snow accumulations over higher terrain above the Snake River Valley and the Blue Mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer near Asotin recorded 7.5 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning the afternoon of February 14th and continuing through the night into the morning of the 15th a moist warm front moved south to north through eastern Washington. Initial light snow changed over to rain with the warm front passage in the Columbia Basin and the Cascades East Slopes during the evening hours with generally 1 to locally 4 inch snow accumulations before this change-over. The warm air tongue did not penetrate the northeastern mountains during the night with heavy snow accumulations noted in Stevens and Pend Oreille Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 8 miles south of Northport recorded 6.8 inches of new snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ponding was reported along West Forth and South Steptoe Street with water up to the bumpers on vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of marginal instability and strong shear brought strong to severe thunderstorms to Klickitat and Benton County producing hail up to nickel size near Goldendale and wind gusts up to 54 knots (62 mph) west of the Hanford Site on the afternoon of June 26th. This event was covered by a severe thunderstorm watch, the last watch that was issued for the Inland Northwest occurred 780 days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported at Brooks Memorial Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold Arctic front settled into the region mid to late October, setting the stage for an early winter weather scenario. As a secondary storm system and cold front interacted with the mountains of southeast Washington, moderate to heavy snow developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Touchet SNOTEL site recorded 10.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough swung down from British Columbia, carrying with it a surface frontal system. Colder air behind the front combined with an unstable environment lead to the development of convective post-frontal showers. The mountains received the first snowfall accumulation of the season. A winter weather advisory had been issued for this event, however, with it being the first significant (i.e., with travel impacts) accumulating snowfall of the season, snowfall totals actually met winter storm warning criteria for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No significant snowfall accumulation was noted at Hurricane Ridge. The day prior to the event (October 7th), Hurricane Ridge received 4 inches of snow. Snow depth then remained consistent through the day October 8th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough swung down from British Columbia, carrying with it a surface frontal system. Colder air behind the front combined with an unstable environment lead to the development of convective post-frontal showers. The mountains received the first snowfall accumulation of the season. A winter weather advisory had been issued for this event, however, with it being the first significant (i.e., with travel impacts) accumulating snowfall of the season, snowfall totals actually met winter storm warning criteria for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to pickup after 6am at Stevens Pass. Roughly 4 inches of snow accumulated between 7-9am with another 5 inches by noon. Accumulation leveled off after that through the 9th. Total snowfall accumulation of about 10.5 inches was measured in 12 hours at Stevens Pass on the 8th. Snow also began falling at Snoqualmie Pass around 6am and continued through mid afternoon. Accumulation of just over 4 inches was reported at 3pm. Precip/snowfall tapered off after that with no additional accumulation of note, bringing a total snowfall accumulation of just over 4 inches in 12 hours at Snoqualmie Pass.

A winter weather advisory had been issued for this event, however, with it being the first significant (i.e., with travel impacts) accumulating snowfall of the season, snowfall totals actually met winter storm warning criteria for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall appears to have started the previous evening, around 5-6pm, with around 15 inches of snow reported by 9pm. Snow then appeared to taper off through the overnight hours and through the 8th with no notable additional accumulation. For this event, Mount Baker Ski area received around 15 inches of snow during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable southerly flow over eastern Washington ahead of an offshore upper level low pressure promoted the development of thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 26th over eastern Washington and the Idaho Panhandle. While numerous reports of 45 to 55 mph wind gusts and some reports of dime sized hail were received, a few of these storms over southeastern Washington became severe with reports of downed trees and power lines in the Lewiston/Clarkston area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported large tree branches and power lines down in Clarkston.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern took shape across the Pacific Northwest, with several shortwave troughs bringing in several rounds of precipitation (the strongest shortwave passed through on the 19th). The precipitation fell in the mountains as snow, with enough accumulation to warrant winter weather headlines. Headlines were issued well in advance of this storm (Oct 17).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate at Stevens Pass on the evening of the 18th with 3.5 inches of new snow by midnight. An additional 5.5 inches of snow then fell on the 19th before tapering off early in the day on the 20th. The total snowfall accumulation at Stevens Pass was around 9 inches of snow (roughly between 5pm on the 18th and midnight on the 19th). Snow did not begin at Snoqualmie Pass until around 7am on the 19th due to temps above freezing. Snoqualmie received about 3 inches of snow through late afternoon. Total snowfall accumulation at Snoqualmie Pass for this event of around 3 inches from roughly 7 AM on the 19th thru 5 PM on the 19th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough swung down from British Columbia, carrying with it a surface frontal system. Colder air behind the front combined with an unstable environment lead to the development of convective post-frontal showers. The mountains received the first snowfall accumulation of the season. A winter weather advisory had been issued for this event, however, with it being the first significant (i.e., with travel impacts) accumulating snowfall of the season, snowfall totals actually met winter storm warning criteria for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began around 9am at Paradise with 5.5 inches noted by about 5pm. An additional few tenths of an inch then fell through mid evening before tapering off. Total snowfall accumulation at Paradise of just under 6 inches (5.8 inches) in 12 hours. Crystal Mountain snowfall started around 9am with just over 6 inches reported 12 hours later before tapering. No significant accumulation was found at White Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped south along the coast from Victoria Island. The low pressure system brought arctic air south out of Canada into SW Washington. This system brought heavy snow to the Willapa Hills, Cascade Foothills, and the Columbia Gorge. It also brought snow down to the Valley floor in the Vancouver Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The south Washington Cascade Foothills observed anywhere from 6 to 12 inches of snow.  A weather station east of Chelatchie reported 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Back-to-back low pressure systems dropping south along the coast of British Columbia and Washington brought cold air south into SW Washington as well as plenty of moisture.  Locations in the Columbia Gorge saw 1 to 2 feet of snow.  At lower elevations, a near stationary frontal boundary Monday night into Tuesday morning dropped several inches of rain over portions of Cowlitz County causing flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest amounts were at higher elevations.  The Calamity SNOTEL recorded 12 inches of snow, while locations in Yacolt and Amboy only reported around 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Back-to-back low pressure systems dropping south along the coast of British Columbia and Washington brought cold air south into SW Washington as well as plenty of moisture.  Locations in the Columbia Gorge saw 1 to 2 feet of snow.  At lower elevations, a near stationary frontal boundary Monday night into Tuesday morning dropped several inches of rain over portions of Cowlitz County causing flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exact amounts of snow and ice are unknown due to lack of reports. One spotter report from Stevenson had 7.5 inches of snow in a 12 hour period. Another report from the public in Stevenson had 10 inches of snow in a 24 hour period. Heavy snow and ice closed I-84 in both directions east of Milepost 23 due to heavy snow and ice which caused a crash involving multiple tractor-trailers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both ramps of I-5 northbound at Kalama exit 30 were closed and under water. Many other roads in the area were under water.  Rain and runoff also created problems for Kalama���s water treatment plant, prompting the city to shut the plant down due to high turbidity from slides along Kalama River Road.

A trained spotter in Longview, WA at 750 feet reported 7.5 inches of snow. Another trained spotter in Kalama reported 7.5 inches of snow at an elevation of 1350 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving up from the southwest brought moisture up the east side of the Cascades, wrapping around into the Columbia Gorge. This combined with cold air pulled into the Columbia Gorge from the Columbia Basin brought heavy snow to the Columbia Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer reported 5.8 inches over a 24 hour period near Cascade Locks. Locations along the Washington side had CoCoRaHS reports of 3 to 5 inches of snow.

Reports came from various networks including CoCoRaHS, trained spotters, and from our COOP observer at Bonneville Dam. Per the Oregonian (newspaper), Interstate 84 was closed in both directions east of Milepost 23 at 528 AM on February 12th due to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 4th, a brief, weak tornado touched down near Tenino, WA. No damage or impacts were reported from this event. However, it was caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 10/04/2019 a brief weak tornado was confirmed by video and fire/rescue report near Tenino, WA. No impacts or damage were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between approximately 6:02 - 6:07 PM on the evening of October 18, a tornado touched down near Shelton in Mason County, Washington before moving over the water of Pickering Passage and dissipating as a waterspout. The first video evidence and reports from area fire officials prompted the NWS to send a damage survey team to the area during the afternoon of October 19, 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between approximately 6:02 - 6:07 PM on the evening of October 18, a tornado touched down near Shelton in Mason County, Washington and traveled approximately 0.68 miles before moving over the water of Pickering Passage and dissipating as a waterspout. The first video evidence and reports from area fire officials prompted the NWS to send a damage survey team to the area during the afternoon of October 19, 2019. Preliminary evaluation of the damage by the team concluded that the tornado reached an EF-1 rating. The damage path was up to 90 yards in width and the tornado was estimated to be on the ground for approximately 5 minutes.||Fortunately, no injuries were reported with this tornado. Most of the damage was in the form of uprooted or snapped soft wood fir and cedar trees. Falling trees did impact at least two houses along the damage path, including one house that had two trees fall on the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It started as a low pressure system moving south into eastern Washington, bringing a cold front southeastward across western Oregon and southwest Washington. The front lifted north as a surface low pressure system rode up the stationary front and brought another surge of moisture and heavy snow on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 6 inches of snow fell in the central Columbia River Gorge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through eastern Washington during the afternoon of October 25th. Windy conditions developed along and behind this cold front. Generally winds of 15 to 25 mph were observed with gusts in the 30 to 40 mph range, however a few gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range were recorded in and near the Cascades, mostly at exposed high elevation automated weather stations. There were localized reports of tree damage in the Spokane area. Near the Cascades where terrain channeling may have promoted locally higher gusts tree damage was reported in the Leavenworth area where a tree fell on two homes and injured three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local strong wind gusts toppled trees into power lines on Spokane's South Hill with local power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust topped a tree onto two homes in Leavenworth. Three people in the homes were reported injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of October 28th a strong arctic cold front descended out of Canada and into eastern Washington. This cold dense air squeezed through northern valley gaps and into the Columbia Basin with winds generally 20 to 30 mph with gusts in the 35 to 45 mph range with the highest local gust recorded at 59 mph at a highway sensor near Washtucna.||An intense snow squall developed along this front during the evening hours which created near zero visibility in blowing snow along highway 195 in southern Spokane County and Whitman County leading to the temporary closure of this major north-south route due to accidents and slick road conditions.||The winds also contributed to the rapid spread of a wildfire near Rock Island in Douglas County. This 15 acre fire destroyed a barn and two parked vehicles before being brought under control.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Webb Place Wildfire began in an orchard near Rock Island due to unknown causes. Gusty winds caused the fire to quickly spread to consume 15 acres. While not particularly large this fire killed 40 chickens as it consumed a barn and damaged two vehicles parked nearby. Firefighters extinguished the blaze as winds died down later in the evening.

This 15 acre fire destroyed a barn and two parked vehicles before being brought under control.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous auto accidents were reported by law enforcement on Highway 195 between Spokane and Spangle due to low visibility and icy conditions as a snow squall passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful shortwave trough and associated cold front swept over the Cascades during the day on the 25th. This lead to strong to severe gap winds affecting mainly the Kittitas Valley in central Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph gust was measured at the Ellensburg Airport. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful shortwave trough and associated cold front swept over the Cascades during the day on the 25th. This lead to strong to severe gap winds affecting mainly the Kittitas Valley in central Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports a 5 foot diameter tree fell on a home, causing roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho with cold and unstable conditions promoting localized heavy snow showers. During the late evening of October 8th and into the early morning hours of October 9th persistent but localized snow showers formed over the South Hill neighborhood of Spokane area and extended into the Coeur D'Alene area of north Idaho. Three to 5 inches of snow accumulation was reported from this narrow area with local accumulations to 6 inches. Generally 1 to 2 inches of snow fell in surrounding areas outside of this localized maximum.||This event occurred before most deciduous trees had shed their leaves for the season resulting in multiple reports of broken branches and a few toppled trees from the weight of the snow. Over 32,000 utility customers lost power from branches breaking onto power lines and the Spokane Public School system declared a snow day on October 9th due to travel disruptions and power failures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported 32,234 customers without power mainly in the Spokane South Hill neighborhood due to snow laden trees and branches falling into power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 18th the Cold Creek Wildfire began just west of the Hanford Site and burned 45000 acres primarily on the Arid Lands Ecology Reserve and Rattlesnake Mountain. This fire was determined to be human caused.

During the evening of July 23rd, a strong upper level trough triggered thunderstorms in a moist and unstable air mass over northeast Oregon. These storms moved northeastward into eastern Washington and north Idaho forming into a line of thunderstorms featuring severe wind gusts as they passed through the region. Numerous reports of 60 mph wind gusts, downed trees and power outages were received from northeast Washington and north Idaho during the late evening hours as this line swept through the region. Lightning and downed power lines caused multiple brush and structure fires around the area. An estimated 30,000 customers lost power during this thunderstorm outbreak in northeast Washington and north Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received of downed trees, power outages and damaged vehicles from thunderstorm winds in the South Hill neighborhood of Spokane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 2nd numerous slow moving thunderstorms containing heavy rain formed near the center of an upper level low pressure over the western Columbia Basin and the east slopes of the Cascade Mountains. A rich moisture feed wrapping around this upper low pressure allowed some of these storms to produce heavy rainfall over limited areas. A spotter near Waterville reported 1.29 inches of rain from a series of thunderstorms training through the area. Several reports of flash flooding were received from the region with multiple roads blocked by flowing water, mud and rocks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding caused mud to flow over Highway 2 east of Waterville. The road was fully blocked by mud for about one hour while highway crews worked to clear the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 2nd numerous slow moving thunderstorms containing heavy rain formed near the center of an upper level low pressure over the western Columbia Basin and the east slopes of the Cascade Mountains. A rich moisture feed wrapping around this upper low pressure allowed some of these storms to produce heavy rainfall over limited areas. A spotter near Waterville reported 1.29 inches of rain from a series of thunderstorms training through the area. Several reports of flash flooding were received from the region with multiple roads blocked by flowing water, mud and rocks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood swept across Highway 97A 4 miles north of Entiat. Water and mud was reported by local news media to be up to 2 feet deep. Two trucks were reported stuck in the mud. The road was closed for about 24 hours while highway crews worked to clear the mud and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 2nd numerous slow moving thunderstorms containing heavy rain formed near the center of an upper level low pressure over the western Columbia Basin and the east slopes of the Cascade Mountains. A rich moisture feed wrapping around this upper low pressure allowed some of these storms to produce heavy rainfall over limited areas. A spotter near Waterville reported 1.29 inches of rain from a series of thunderstorms training through the area. Several reports of flash flooding were received from the region with multiple roads blocked by flowing water, mud and rocks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on Slide Ridge above South Lakeshore Drive near Chelan washed mud and debris across the road to a depth of 10 to 12 feet in places. The road was closed for a week while highway crews cleared the road and stabilized the hill side.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 150 between Chelan and Manson was closed due to a small mudslide caused by heavy rain. The road re-opened after a few hours when highway crews cleared the mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 5th thunderstorms formed over northeast Oregon and moved  into extreme southeast Washington and the southern Idaho Panhandle. Scattered reports of trees down and power outages were received from these areas before these storms dissipated later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire was reported near Silcott caused by power lines blown down by thunderstorm winds. Time estimated from radar data. The local Utility Company reported 2400 customers without power in the Lewiston and Clarkston area as a result of thunderstorm winds bringing down power lines in and around the cities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 23rd, a strong upper level trough triggered thunderstorms in a moist and unstable air mass over northeast Oregon. These storms moved northeastward into eastern Washington and north Idaho forming into a line of thunderstorms featuring severe wind gusts as they passed through the region. Numerous reports of 60 mph wind gusts, downed trees and power outages were received from northeast Washington and north Idaho during the late evening hours as this line swept through the region. Lightning and downed power lines caused multiple brush and structure fires around the area. An estimated 30,000 customers lost power during this thunderstorm outbreak in northeast Washington and north Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station at the Spokane Felts Field Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern on August 10th featured a very moist and unstable air mass with a passing upper level low pressure. These elements promoted widespread thunderstorms with heavy rain which produced Flash Flooding over potions of eastern Washington. Local observations indicated some of these slow moving storms produced from 1 to locally in excess of 5 inches of rain. Particularly hard hit was the Wenatchee area where flash flooding was reported in gulches and canyons emptying into the city. These floods produced severe damage to at least three mobile homes in Wenatchee.  ||Elsewhere heavy rains flooded sections of Highway 2 on the Waterville Plateau and the area around Elk and Chatteroy north of Spokane received up to 5 inches of rain from a stationary thunderstorm which produced scattered flooding of streets and driveways, water in basements and washouts on hillsides. There were also isolated reports of hail with diameters of up to 1 inch with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm eroded a culvert and hillside above the Squilchuck Mobile Home Park resulting in three mobile homes being buried up to their roof lines with mud and rocks.  Residents in 16 other nearby homes were advised to evacuate until the hillside stabilized.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets in the southern portion of Wenatchee were reported as flooded and residents were advised to avoid travel in the area. Mud and debris flows in the Squilchuck, Pritcher and Halverson Canyons flowed down these canyons and into the city. In addition heavy rain in the city of Wenatchee backed up storm drains and sewers and contributed to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters on the Williams Flats Wildfire reported numerous roads washed out due to heavy rain from a series of thunderstorms passing over the burn area. They were successfully extracted the next morning using an alternate route.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper storm system combined with modest low and mid level moisture to yield scattered strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Storms developed first across the higher terrain of central south-central Washington and northern Oregon near the Cascades, then lifted northward, producing large hail and strong winds along the way.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall accompanied the hail with totals of 2.5 to 3 inches in the same location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low drifted eastward into Oregon and Washington on the 10th. Modest instability and adequate moisture lead to afternoon thunderstorm development, resulting in a few severe reports and at least one cold air funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of August 2nd lightning ignited a wildfire near Keller. The Williams Flat Fire burned 44,446 acres of uninhabited timber land before being fully contained on August 25th. No structures or homes were lost but residents in a nearby development were advised to evacuate in case the fire cut off their access road.||During the evening of August 10th thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the fire site with numerous roads washed out and 64 firefighters temporarily stranded on higher ground when a their access road washed out from a flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Williams Flats Fire started on August 2, 2019 after a band of early morning thunderstorms with lightning moved across the eastern portion of the Colville Indian Reservation. The fire burned south of Silver Creek Road, within the designated Game Reserve. The fire was contained by August 25th. It burned 44,446 acres of timber and brush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the evening of December 18th through the morning of December 20th  a deep fetch of Pacific moisture was directed into the region. This moisture enhanced along a warm front and produced heavy snow across the mountains of eastern Washington. In the lower elevations the warm front quickly raised snow levels over the eastern Columbia Basin and the valleys of north Idaho limiting snow accumulations to 1 to 3 inches before changing over to rain. In the western Columbia Basin cold air dammed against the Cascades was slow to scrub out and precipitation remained as snow for a longer period resulting in heavy accumulations in the Wenatchee area, the Waterville Plateau and the Okanogan Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Lake Wenatchee recorded 17.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front developed over central Washington during the day of December 1st. Light snow fell over the Columbia Basin including the Interstate 90 corridor. The snow apparently melted upon the road surface and then froze into a layer of ice. Actual snow accumulations during this event were generally less than 1 inch across the region. ||Over 25 collisions occurred on I-90 in Central Washington between George and Moses Lake closing eastbound I-90 from shortly before 4:30 PM til 7:30 PM on December 1st. Road conditions began to deteriorate after 2 pm when snow began to fall. A series of collisions, mostly consisting of rollovers and vehicles sliding off the roadway, led to the closure of eastbound I-90 at MP154 with most of the collisions occurring between MP161 and MP164. There were at least two people sent to the hospital for minor injuries. A detour was in place, but the narrow road led to extensive back-ups. Moses Lake ASOS had visibility drop as low was 3/4 of a mile during the snow event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Basin Herald reported over 25 collisions including slide-offs and some roll-overs on interstate 90 between George and Moses Lake. The Eastbound lane of the interstate was closed between 4:30 PM PST and 7:30 PM. Two minor injuries to motorists were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass over eastern Washington produced scattered slow moving heavy rain bearing thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of May 16th. Rainfall rates up to 0.9 inches in 20 minutes were observed with rainfall totals around one inch between 4:00 PM and 8:00 PM in and around downtown Spokane. Lesser amounts were observed outside of this area. ||The most intense storm occurred between 4:30 PM and 5:30 PM over the the downtown commercial district and north Spokane residential area and produced widespread urban flooding which severely disrupted the evening commute with impassable or restricted intersections from deep ponding of water on the roads. Emergency crews made several water rescues of stranded motorists from cars stalled in 8 to 12 inches of water at one intersection in north Spokane.||A funnel cloud was photographed by a member of the public from another nearby thunderstorm earlier in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public photographed a small funnel cloud north of Interstate 90 over uninhabited land in Lincoln County near the border with Spokane County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 1st a dust devil that formed near the Zillah High School in Zillah Washingon, Yakima County. Five injuries (direct) including one critical injury were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to media reports a bounce house was lofted up to 20 feet in the air and thrown up to 240 feet from its original position by localized strong winds. Video from the aftermath shows a dust devil in the background. Winds from a nearby mesonet station were less than 20 mph. Five youths inside the bounce house were injured, 1 critically.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of southwest Washington. The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds observed in the southwest interior weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded sustained winds up to 51 mph with gusts up to 69 mph. Another weather station on Megler Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 49 mph with gusts up to 65 mph.  Ocean Park also saw wind gusts up to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of southwest Washington. The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds observed in the southwest interior weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations recorded wind gusts up to 40 to 45 mph, with stronger winds recorded just south at the Portland Airport, which recorded wind gusts up to 54 mph. Most impacts were observed closer to the Columbia River in Vancouver. At one point, Clark County PUD said close to 100,000 customers lost power. A power failure in Clark County triggered a sewage spill near the bridge spanning Salmon Creek. Two NWS employees had trees blow down in their yards in Vancouver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low drifted over the northern Cascades during the afternoon hours of the 10th. This brought cold air aloft, leading to spotty showers and even a stray thunderstorm or two. At least one funnel cloud was reported near Ellensburg in the Kittitas Valley. The funnel did not appear to reach ground and no damage was reported. While other storms occurred, none were especially strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures on social media depicting a funnel cloud. No ground circulation confirmed and no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved onshore from the Pacific during the afternoon of the 7th. This coupled with modest moisture, instability, and upslope flow, allowed for spotty shower and thunderstorm development in the afternoon. At least one thunderstorm produced severe hail near Goldendale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged in size from dimes to quarters. Time estimated from radar.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Arctic front swept through the Inland Northwest late in the month, bringing an stretch of record cold and even some wintry precipitation. At least one fatality occurred as a result of heavy snow in one of the mountain passes of central Washington north of Ellensburg. Several vehicle accidents occurred with one confirmed fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snowfall coupled with melting/refreezing snow lead to treacherous travel conditions near Blewett Pass. This resulted in at least 9 automobile collisions and one fatality in the evening and overnight hours of the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of southwest Washington. The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds observed in the southwest interior weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Abernathy RAWS recorded sustained winds up to 53 mph with gusts up to 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of December 10th through the morning of December 12th a warm front and a following cold front passage delivered heavy snow accumulations to the East Slopes of the Cascades in Eastern Washington. The central portion of eastern Washington received only light amounts of snow benefiting from a precipitation shadow in the lee of the Cascades. The rising terrain of far eastern Washington and the northeastern mountains received generally 2 to 4 inches of snow but there were isolated pockets of heavier amounts over extreme northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Lake Wenatchee received 4.0 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the early morning hours of December 31st and lasting into the morning of January 1st, 2020 a moist northwesterly flow off the Pacific enhanced over a warm front into widespread snow over the Northeast Mountains and the northeastern Columbia Basin of far eastern Washington including the Spokane area. Heavy snow accumulations were noted in these areas during the morning hours before temperatures increased to above freezing and began melting the accumulation. While lighter amounts were noted across the Okanogan Highlands, the orientation of the flow promoted a precipitation shadow over the Cascades and areas in the western and central Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in northwest Spokane recorded 4.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern across the Pacific Northwest was responsible for heavy snowfall across the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling in the northern Cascades on the afternoon of the 11th, accumulating 28 inches by the morning of the 14th. Some light snow lingered into the morning of the 15th with little additional impact.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern across the Pacific Northwest was responsible for heavy snowfall across the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the central Cascades during the late afternoon/early evening hours on the 11th, with snow tapering during the morning hours of the 15th. This event affected the Passes, with Stevens Pass receiving around 22 inches of new snow and Snoqualmie Pass receiving around 14 inches of new snow with temperatures right around freezing. Given the marginal temperatures at Snoqualmie Pass, snow ended earlier than Stevens Pass with most accumulating snowfall finished during the morning hours of the 14th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern across the Pacific Northwest was responsible for heavy snowfall across the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling on the evening of the 10th, with heaviest accumulation on the 12th and 13th. Total snow accumulation at Paradise of around 34 inches. Snowfall amounts near White Pass were slightly lower around 25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy snow impacted the |Cascades and the Olympics. Snow began affecting the mountains on the 18th, with several additional periods of heavy snowfall bringing additional accumulation through the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected the Olympics mountains, beginning during the evening hours of the 18th and continuing through the 21st. Snowfall measurement data was intermittent through the event, with estimated new storm total snowfall accumulation of around 30 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Rice reported 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of December 10th through the morning of December 12th a warm front and a following cold front passage delivered heavy snow accumulations to the East Slopes of the Cascades in Eastern Washington. The central portion of eastern Washington received only light amounts of snow benefiting from a precipitation shadow in the lee of the Cascades. The rising terrain of far eastern Washington and the northeastern mountains received generally 2 to 4 inches of snow but there were isolated pockets of heavier amounts over extreme northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Peshastin received 5.1 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds on the evening of November 27th downed trees in Selah and caused Blizard conditions near Benton City, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Blizzard Conditions reported by law enforcement 10 S Benton City. Snow drifts several feet deep. 6 vehicles stranded and  occupants rescued by sheriff deputies and fire first responders in the area of Sellards and Travis roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds on the evening of November 27th downed trees in Selah and caused Blizard conditions near Benton City, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakima Herald reports tress knocked down onto power lines in the 9000 block of Duffields Road and the 500 block of fourth in Selah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over Oregon created sustained and intense upslope snow showers driving into portions of the East Slopes of the Cascades on November 27th and 28th. In general reporting stations in this zone which stretches from Canada to the Wenatchee area recorded 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation during this period, however the ridges just south of Wenatchee on the southern edge of the zone were favorably aligned with the upslope wind flow for a sustained period resulting in up to 23 inches of new snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Wheeler SNOTEL site recorded 22.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure system over central Oregon produced a very strong northeasterly pressure gradient across eastern Washington and north Idaho on November 27th. Numerous Mesonet weather stations through out the region recorded wind gusts of between 40 and 50 mph, however winds funneling through terrain constrictions in northeast Washington locally downed trees and caused power outages. An estimated 2000 residents of eastern Washington and north Idaho lost power during this wind storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down due to high wind gusts in the Newman Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure system over central Oregon produced a very strong northeasterly pressure gradient across eastern Washington and north Idaho on November 27th. An estimated 2000 residents of eastern Washington and north Idaho lost power during this wind storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and a power outage was noted along Forest Road 5303 near Caldwell Lake in Pend Oreille County due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakly unstable air mass over eastern Washington during the afternoon of November 26th promoted isolated snow showers over the eastern Columbia Basin. One of these snow showers became locally intense as it crossed Interstate 90 from south to north just to the west of Spokane. Multiple collisions involving up to 127 vehicles resulted on this stretch of road as drivers unexpectedly encountered about 1/2 inch of snow with a thin layer of ice on the road surface and sharply reduced visibility. Five people were injured in these pile-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington State Patrol reported numerous multi-car collisions on a stretch of Interstate 90 between Spokane and Medical Lake due to a local intense snow shower passing across the highway between 2:05 PM PST and 2:25 PM. The shower deposited about 1/2 inch of snow which quickly turned to a thin layer of ice as it was compacted by traffic. Up to 127 vehicles were involved in 35 separate crashes along this ice coated stretch of road. Five motorists were injured in these collisions. The accidents were cleared and the road fully re-opened to traffic around 6:30 PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mission Ridge Ski Resort reported an estimated 23.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 28th and 29th a cold and deep upper level low descended into the region from Canada. A persistent band of wrap around precipitation began during the day of September 28th and continued into the morning of September 29th impacting mostly the Northeast Mountains and Okanogan highlands zones. During the late afternoon and overnight hours of September 28th precipitation from this system fell as early season accumulating snow in the valleys of northeastern Washington. Heavy wet snow caused broken tree limbs on trees which had not shed their leaves. Gusty northeast winds aggravated the situation by breaking tree branches and in some cases toppling heavy snow laden trees around the region.  Numerous power outages occurred. ||The mountains and higher elevation valleys of northeast Washington received their first heavy snow accumulations of the year from this system. The highest amount reported occurred above 6000 feet in the north Cascades with a spotter on Cooper Mountain measuring 26.5 inches of snow from this storm, however the valleys of the East Slopes of the Cascades received only minor accumulations. ||The hills and benches around the Spokane area received light to moderate snow accumulations which also contributed to scattered power outages from broken tree branches into power lines with over 1500 customers affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road leading up to Mount Spokane was closed due to fallen trees from heavy wet snow and gusty winds. At the lower elevations an estimated 3 inches of snow was reported as of 3:00 pm September 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From September 8th through September 11th a slow moving upper level low pressure with a shield of wrap around Pacific moisture passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho.  Areas of moderate to locally heavy rain showers and a few thunderstorms ranged through out the region during this cool and unstable wet period. Locally heavy rain caused a few Flash Floods and numerous Debris Flows in the Cascades east slopes region and some local flooding near Pullman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain from a thunderstorm pooled at an intersection in downtown Pullman apparently due to leaf clogged storm drains. The water pooled about 9 inches deep in the intersection which was sufficient to cause minor first floor flooding in a number of adjacent business establishments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From September 8th through September 11th a slow moving upper level low pressure with a shield of wrap around Pacific moisture passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho.  Areas of moderate to locally heavy rain showers and a few thunderstorms ranged through out the region during this cool and unstable wet period. Locally heavy rain caused a few Flash Floods and numerous Debris Flows in the Cascades east slopes region and some local flooding near Pullman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood occurred on the Entiat River Road northwest of Ardenvior where it passes near the Cougar Creek 2018 burn scar. The flood deposited 6 to 7 feet of mud and debris across the road. A nearby weather spotter measured 1.2 inches of rain fall from the storm believed to have caused this nocturnal flash flood. Time estimated from radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred along South Lakeshore Drive near Manson during the early morning hours of September 10th. The road was buried under 8 feet of mud, rock and debris. A nearby rain gauge recorded 1.17 inches of rain between 4:20 am and 6:20 am PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began in the range land near the town of Okanogan during the afternoon of September 1st. The fire burned 142 acres and damaged vehicles parked in a field near a garage. One firefighter suffered severe burns when a wind gust blew the fire back on him as he was fighting the fire. He was immediately evacuated for treatment but eventually died of these burns on October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spring Coulee Fire began from an unknown cause during the afternoon of September 1st two miles west of the town of Okanogan. The fire was quickly contained after burning 142 acres of range land and damaging some vehicles. One firefighter was severely burned while fighting the fire and eventually died of his injuries on October 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Basin of eastern Washington is a dry basin under the rain shadow of the Cascade Mountains. Consequently this area is an open range land of grass and sage which quickly dries out and becomes vulnerable to wildfire from late spring through early autumn.  Breezy downslope winds off of the nearby Cascade mountains are common and promote rapid spreading of wildfires in the basin. ||The 243 Command Fire started on Monday night, June 3, near Highway 243 and Wanapum Dam. Winds pushed the fire easterly through the Lower Crab Creek Canyon of southern Grant County. Fire crews were able to contain it between the canyon���s northern and southern ridges. The fire burned 20,380 acres of mostly range and scrub land. No structure or homes were lost but some power lines and infrastructure were lost. There were no injuries reported. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 243 Command Fire started June 3, near Highway 243 and Wanapum Dam. Breezy winds pushed the fire easterly through the Lower Crab Creek Canyon. The fire crews were able to contain it between the canyon���s northern and southern ridges. The fire burned 20,380 acres of range land.
